label: Heysham Half Moon Bay
comment: On Friday 27th April a third party contractor investigating a brown field site accidentally drilled through a United Utilities rising main sewer on the corner of St George’s Quay and Lune Road.  United Utilities isolated the damaged section of sewer and arranged a fleet of nine tankers to collect sewage from the pumping stations and transport it to Lancaster WwTW.  Following rainfall on Monday 30th April United Utilities reported on Tuesday 1st May that they were unable to keep on top of flows and a continuous discharge of 20 l/s was being made from Riverside Pumping Station.  The repair was made by United Utilities on 5th May by replacing the damaged rising main sewer section.

The repair was made by United Utilities on 5th May by replacing the damaged rising main sewer section.

label: Morecambe South
comment: The long sea outfall from Morecambe Wastewater Treatment Works operated by United Utilities is partially blocked by sand restricting the flow of treated effluent which can be made to sea.  The excess flow is being stored within storm sewage storage tanks at Schola Green Pumping Station.  These storage tanks are nearing capacity and this is resulting in elevated levels of storm sewage being discharged through the outfall serving Schola Green Pumping Station.  This has the potential to reduce the bathing water quality at Morecambe South and Morecambe North bathing waters.

label: East Runton
comment: There was discharge of untreated sewage onto East Runton beach, the duration and quantity is being investigated but it is likely that this had taken place for over 24 hours, before the discharge was stopped on 12/08/14 at approximately 13:00. Monitoring of the sea water has confirmed that there is no further impact.

label: Pevensey Bay
comment: The local water company reported damage to the long sea outfall at Eastbourne on Friday 15 May 2015. Whilst repairing the damage, sewage was discharged via an alternate outfall closer to the shore. The water company successfully fixed the long-sea outfall and the treatment works was functioning normally from 18:30 on 17 May 2015. Bathing water samples collected on Saturday 16 May were of good quality. Due to the length of time passed since the full repair, the dispersion and dilution from tide and wind mixing that will have occurred and the samples showing good quality at each bathing water, the abnormal situation can now be removed.

We are working with the emergency services to minimise the environmental impact from this incident. The abnormal situation was lifted on the 20/06/14,  Local experts deciding that potential bacterial contamination from the incident had been given sufficient time to reduce to acceptable levels.

label: Kingsand
comment:
On Saturday 15th June 2013 the Environment Agency received an incident report from a member of the public who reported sewage entering a watercourse and onto the beach (NIRS 1122647). Further calls from the public followed. On contacting South West Water Services Limited (SWWSL), regarding the reports, it was revealed that they became aware of it the day before and had sent a team of their contractors to investigate and resolve. It appears that the team left site on Friday evening having been unable to solve the issue but failed to let anyone know and that they would return again on Saturday to try again. 
Following the report the EM Duty Officer sent out a Site Controller to investigate. They reported seeing sewage discharging out of the sewer in 3 separate locations into the watercourse which flowed down onto the beach before discharging into the sea. However no visible sewage debris was detected on the beach itself.
With the incident on-going and the statutory sample scheduled to be taken later that day, the decision to declare an Abnormal Situation (AS) was made. The process as laid out in OI 140_07 ver 10 was then followed. The local authority arranged for signage to be erected to warn the public of the issue by rangers from Mount Edgecombe Park who are responsible for the beach. SWW were also informed by phone and email. SWW advised the Agency the problem of the sewer blockage was resolved late Saturday afternoon. The statutory bathing water sample was coded to make it investigational.
On Monday 17th June, the results of the stream and beach samples taken on Saturday came back from the labs: Beach: <10 e.coli/100ml; Stream: 30,000 e.coli/100ml
A member of the Sampling & Collection team returned to the site on Monday to ensure the problem had stopped and take further samples. Whilst on site another incident, this time a discharging manhole flowing into the sea via a surface drain was noted in close vicinity to the surface pipe serving the watercourse. Initially it was thought this was linked to the initial incident but subsequent investigations revealed it was a separate problem impacting on the beach. Further samples of the beach and stream were taken.
On Tuesday 18th June, Agency staff once more attended site and fresh samples of the stream and beach were taken. SWW confirmed that the second incident was due to a sheared bolt which reduced the efficacy of one of its pumping stations close to the foreshore, resulting in a sewage spill out of a manhole and ultimately onto the beach and that the problem was now resolved.
On Wednesday 19th June the results from the samples collected on the Monday came in: Beach 2300 e.coli/100ml; Stream >100,000 e.coli per 100ml. Based on these results, produced from potentially 2 separate sources, the AS remained in place.
On Thursday 20th June results taken from Tuesdays sampling came in: Beach 27 e.coli/100ml; Stream 1081 e.coli/100ml. Based on these results the AS was lifted on Thursday 20th June 2013 and the decision relayed to the required parties.

label: Polkerris
comment: The sewage treatment process at St Austell North STW is not working properly. As a consequence it is likely that the final effluent will be reduced in quality making the UV reduction of bacteria low prior to discharge into the Par river.  The works is approximately 7km from Par Beach and is also close to Polkerris Beach.

label: Wembury
comment: South West Water reported an incident to the Environment Agency regarding a sewage pumping station failure close to Wembury beach near Plymouth. Sewage was discharging into a local stream and then into the sea. Only liquid as the discharge was screened. Repairs are being carried out to the pumping station. Tankers and back up pumps are in use. There were no visible signs of contamination as the tide was in and was going out during this discharge.

label: Dawlish Town
comment:
The Environment Agency has received a report from South West Water of a problem at one of its pumping stations. Holcombe (Smugglers Inn) PS has experienced an unusually high amount of ragging which has prevented the pumps from working. As a result there have been intermittent screened discharges of sewage into the sea. The pumping station is close to 3 designated bathing waters – Teignmouth (Holcombe), Teignmouth (Coryton Cove) and Dawlish Town.
The water company have been working to resolve the problem.  The Local Authority (Teignbridge District Council) has been informed as have South West Water of the decision taken on 15/05/13 to declare an Abnormal Situation and to suspend statutory sampling of the three adjacent bathing waters. Investigative sampling by the Environment Agency is underway to assist in the assessment as to when normal compliance sampling can resume.

label: Dawlish Town
comment: A discharge of sewage occurred into Dawlish Water from the Combined Sewerage Overflow (CSO) in Brook Street due to a blockage in the sewer network.  The Environment Agency received the notification from South West Water at 13:58 on Friday 28th August, and were concerned about the potential impact on Dawlish Town Beach.  Signs warning the public were erected around where Dawlish water enters the sea, and  the location of the bathing water. The blockage was cleared and the discharge ceased at 15:48 on Friday 28th August. As a precaution the monitoring suspension remained in place until Saturday 29/08/15 at 16:00.

label: Shaldon
comment:
On the 28th June 2013 the Environment Agency received an incident report from South West Water Services Limited (SWWSL), concerning a discharge of sewage due to problems at one of their coastal pumping stations.
The incident was due to a blockage somewhere in the sewer line close to the Milford Park Pumping Station, Teignmouth. No flows were arriving at the pumping station; instead flows were discharging out of a nearby CSO. Some flows were also discharging out of manhole in a car park close to the pumping station. With SWW struggling to resolve the blockage and the CSO discharging close to a number of designated bathing waters, most notably Shaldon, the decision to declare an Abnormal Situation (AS) was made, following discussions with local Analysis & Reporting and Sampling & Collection teams.
The process as laid out in OI 140_07 ver 10 was then followed. The local authority Teignbridge District Council (TDC) took the decision to erect signage not just on Shaldon Beach but also other beaches it felt might be impacted, Teignmouth Town Beach, and Back Beach (not a designated bathing water). Sampling of the beaches were organised by SWW and analysis undertaken at their accredited labs at Countess Wear. 
Later that day, the cause of the blockage was identified. The in line penstock has sheared from the gearbox and fallen into a closed position. Sewage rag then jammed up against it resulting in the blockage. Whilst the penstock issue had been cleared, initially the pumps were only able to operate intermittently due to the heavy build up of rag until this was also cleared. The incident was finally declared over late afternoon on Friday 28th June.
On the morning of Saturday 29th June, the Agency deployed staff to take post incident samples on the 3 beaches. Later that day the results of samples taken by SWW on the Friday were provided to the Agency:
Shaldon Beach: FC 870/ 100ml; TC 3600/100ml
Back Beach: FC 2700/100ml; TC 5400/100ml
Teignmouth Town Beach: FC <10/100ml; TC 60/100ml

Based on the results, TDC took the decision to keep the signage in place until the results of the post incident sampling undertaken by the Agency.
On Sunday 30th June, SWW phoned through the results of Saturdays samples collected at the 3 beaches:
Shaldon Beach: FC <10/ 100ml; TC <10/100ml
Back Beach: FC 83/100ml; TC 260/100ml
Teignmouth Town Beach: FC 10/100ml; TC 20/100ml

Based on these results and discussions with TDC, the decision was made to draw to a close the AS and remove the signage. This was communicated to all relevant parties on Sunday 30th June 2013. Note the beach was last sampled for compliance purposes on Thursday 27th June and it is expected that the AS will not have impacted the taking of the next sample run.
